Definition: Investigations are research programs with defined aims. They can exist at various scales (e.g., they could encompass a grant-funded program of work, the various components comprising a peer-reviewed publication, or a single experiment).
A unique identifier of the dataset.
A human-readable string summarizing the investigation.
Example: The behaviour of laboratory mice in KI-TIERWOHL.

Definition: A human-readable text describing the investigation in more detail.
Example: Research on how mice behave in different environments under laboratory conditions.

Definition: The date on which the dataset was submitted, e.g. with a publication or to a repository.
The date of the first public release of the dataset presently being described.
A license for the reuse of the data associated with this investigation. The Creative Commons licenses cover most use cases and are recommended.
Comments: Discussion: Can multiple licenses be issued per study within one investigation (e.g. multiple partners)? For submission, one license for the published dataset needs to be the "most" restrictive. If the studies licenses are too different, the study has to be published separately as its own investigation. Idea: using SPDX Link https://spdx.dev/

Definition: A placeholder for further adaption of the checklist.
Comments: No MIAPPE avalilable at the moment, only for commentary version- need new name in the future

Definition: An identifier for a literature publication where the investigation is described. Use of DOIs is recommended.
A unique identifier of the study (e.g. a study could be an experiment).
The title should be a representation of the experimental design.
A human-readable text that is describing the study.
Example: Study on the nutritional intake of mice.

Definition: The referred project, the study, the founder, or a collective that is working on this study.
The date and, if relevant, the time when the experiment started.
The date and, if relevant, the time when the experiment ended.
Comments: To be discussed: If animals have long-term experiments without end. Potential splitting of data over longer time periods- letting study open?

Definition: The name and address of the institution responsible for the study.
Example: IEC - Rostock university medical center; https://ror.org/04dm1cm79
Comments: Recommendation to use the ROR. If multiple institutions should be named, then they need to model characteristic institutions to add multiple insitutsions. For a minimal idea, we stick with one contact institution, e.g. the coordination institution, when dealing with larger projects. Another solution would be the split of studies based on institutions.  FAANG also includes for the organization the address, the URI, and the role of the organization.

Definition: The country where the experiment took place, either as a full name or, preferably, as a 2-letter code.
The name of the natural site, experimental facility, laboratory site, external collaborator etc. where the experiment took place.
Example: FBN pig facility; IEC laboratory; farm of Peter

Definition: The latitude of the experimental site in degrees, in decimal format.
Comments: Discussion: Only relevant when there is interaction with the atmosphere (weather). Recommendation to trim the geolocation coordinate uncertainty.

Definition: The longitude of the experimental site in degrees, in decimal format.
Altitude of the experimental site, provided in meters (m).
Comments: Discussion: Remove this here and make it optional for environments where it is necessary.

Definition: A short description of the animal experimental design, possibly including statistical design. In specific cases, e.g. legacy datasets or data computed from several studies, the experimental design can be "unknown"/"NA", "aggregated/reduced data", or simply "none".
Example: In this experiment, 24 animals were studied after statistical evaluation for identifying correlations between the analyzed factors. The experimental setup included 4 groups, each with 6 animals.

Definition: The type of the experimental design of the study, if available, e.g. the kind of statistical analysis, could be shared.
The level of the unit used in the study, e.g. on the level of animal groups, single animals or schwarms.
Comments: Discussion: Originally this always contains label, ID and source (and comment), which is correlated with an ontology. But you can let the ID and source be "empty" ontologies and just use label and comment to get more information about your study design in there.  Ontology in this context could also mean controlled vocabulary or similar things with sources.

Definition: A general description of the observation units in the study and how they were selected, e. g. an animal group of a specific race. If possible, also provide the number of animals in the study.
Example: The observation unit consists of all mice in cage 5 that are siblings and share the same unusual characteristics (e.g. C57BL6/J).

Definition: The type of husbandry in which the study was carried out.
Comments: Discussion: For livestock animals there is the EOL: http://purl.org/obo/owlEOL_0001697. Can it be used even for non-livestock animals, like laboratory mice and primates?

Definition: A short description of the facility in which the study was carried out. An extension of the husbandry facility if needed.
Example: The study contains a mixture of barn and outdoor opportunities by free will of choice.
Comments: Discussion: Is it already covered in siteName? No, just a more detailed description for the husbandry.

Definition: The name of the person (either full name or as used in scientific publications).
Comments: Discussion: In FAANG the personName is split into Last and First Name.

Definition: The electronic mail address of the person.
A unique identifier for the data submitter. If that submitter is an individual, ORCID identifiers are recommended.
The type of contribution of the person to the investigation.
Comments: Discussion: Do we use specific controlled vocabulary? - Using CreDit, e.g. using the list of roles from Zenodo Potential Recommendation: https://datacite-metadata-schema.readthedocs.io/en/4.5/appendices/appendix-1/contributorType/   In FAANG they used the EFO Ontology (EFO_0001440 - for role).

Definition: The institution the person belongs to.
Example: IEC - Rostock university medical center; https://ror.org/04dm1cm79; https://ror.org/0207ad724

Definition: The link to the data file (or the digital object) in a public database (if available). Otherwise, enter a link to a specific data file inside the published investigation.
Comments: Discussion: Is this really just a public link, or is there the possibility to use "internal" links to non-open repositories or even links to files inside the submitted investigation? It could be really just the file path (URI)  - but also "internal" links could be used to make the data accessible, even when a prior request to one of the authors has to be made.

Definition: The description of the format of the data file. Maybe a standard file format name or a description of the organization in which the data was collected in a tabular file.
Example: FASTA; tab-delimited; column headers headers: 1.A 2.B 3.C; desciption_file.txt
Comments: Could use this with fragments (WC3 Standard ARC) because in the plants they do not use a data standard.

Definition: The version of the dataset (the actual data).
Comments: https://www.w3.org/TR/annotation-model/#selectors  This is reasonable for machine actionability & accessibility

Definition: For biological material, we assume it is the first kind of combination used to form a new animal, e.g. an embryo. When there is no information about the lineage/tree (pedigree), the single animal could also be the start of the biological material.  For animals, we have always had a kind of 1:1 connection: mother-father-child. But for plants, there is a 1:n connection - identical seed from the same accession.

An unique identifier for the source material. It could be an internally generated one or one out of the origin country (Botswana). Possible are also a transport number, the prior living circumstances, etc.
An external identifier that already exists, e.g. mouse strains. It's possible that the identifier is identical to the SourceMaterialId.
A unique identifier for the organism at the species level. The use of the NCBI taxon ID is recommended.
The genus name for the organism under study, according to the standard scientific nomenclature.
Species name (formally: specific epithet) for the organism under study, according to standard scientific nomenclature.

The name of any subtaxa level, including variety, crossing name, etc. The category can be used to store any additional taxonomic identifier. To be filled as a key-value pair list format (the key is the name of the rank/category, and the value is the value of the rank/category).
The race of the animals, if available.
The description of a process or treatment prior to the start of the study that equally influences the study. It could be provided as free text or as controlled vocabulary.
Example: quarantine time, prior vaccination, fasting, training
Comments: The description of conditions prior to the start of the experimental protocol (quarantine, fasting, training, etc.).

Definition: The description of the material source could also be an internal name of the source.
Example: There are 10 lab mices of the strain C57BL6/J, all female and over 1 month old.

Definition: The environmental parameters were kept constant throughout the study.  Remark: If animals change locations, e.g., between barns or within the barn, this should be handled across all animals. So, for each observation unit, there could be multiple protocols for a specific condition.
The name of the environment parameters, which are constant within the experiment.
Example: temperature; humidity; kind of barn (conventional, eco, free roaming, cage, pen housing, group housing, etc.); light cycles; flooring; cleaning routine; air circulation

Definition: The value of the environment parameter (defined above), which is constant within the experiment. If possible, use controlled vocabulary.

The object of a study is to ascertain the impact of one or more factors on the animals. Thus, a factor is, by definition, a condition that varies among observation units, which may be biotic (e.g., pest or disease interactions) or abiotic (e.g., treatments and cultural practices). Depending on the level of the data, an experimental factor can be either an action (e.g., overfeeding) or an indirect action, such as a too-high temperature in the summer when outdoors.
The Name or Acronym of the experimental factor.
A description of the experimental factor. This includes all relevant treatments and protocols planned for all animals targeted by a given experimental factor.
Example: For the experiment there was one group with high-fat nutrition and one group with low-fat nutrition feeded.

Definition: A list of possible values for the factor. At least there should be 2 different factor values.

An event is a discrete occurrence at a particular time in the experiment (e.g., natural: rain; unnatural: unplanned veterinary treatment). Events may be the realization of factors or parts of factors, or may be confounding to factors. Can be applied at the whole-study level or only to a subset of observation units. Events are mapped to study protocols and could be used to document issues (very freely).
The short name of the event.
Example: Rearranging animals; vet treating a broken bone; broken equipment (?); sudden death

Definition: The description of the event, including details such as the applied amount or possibly the duration of the event.
Example: Animals are moved from stable A to B; antibiotica for animal X; GPS tracker stopped working at 2024-11-30

Definition: The date and time of the event.

The observation units are objects that are subject to instances of observation and measurement. An observation unit comprises one or more animals and/or their environment. (Synonym: Experimental unit)
A locally unique identifier, used to identify the observation unit in data files containing the values observed or measured on that unit.
Comments: Could this be 1+? For example, you could list just one object, and that unit would be single, or you could list several objects, and the unit would be a group.

Definition: The type of observation unit in textual form, is usually one of the following: single animal, swarm of animals (unsure number), group of animals (known number).
A list of values for each factor applied to the observation unit.

The sex of the biological individual(s) could use controlled vocabulary, e.g., using AGROVOC or the potential differentiation of ABCD: <xs:enumeration value="M"/> <xs:enumeration value="F"/> <xs:enumeration value="U"/> <xs:enumeration value="N"/> <xs:enumeration value="X"/> Normal: male, female, hermaphrodite
The date of birth of the animal (not applicable if there is a group of animals with different dates of birth).
The number of animals per observation unit.

A sample is a portion of an observation unit (observation unit = animal) (e.g., tissue, cells, fluids, hairs). A sample must be used when a physical sample needs to be stored and traced. Otherwise, the observations made at the whole living animal-level (non-invasive) should be recorded as animal level observations using the observed variables to characterize the object of the observation (e.g., physiological properties: length of tail, color of the fur).
A unique identifier for the sample.
Comments: FAANG uses sample names.

Definition: The life cycle stage of the animal source during which the sample was taken.
Example: Over 1 year; adolesence (between 3-6 months); young and old
Comments: Searching if there is a controlled vocabulary.  source (NC3Rs): Prenatal development: 5.5 months Infancy: 0-12 months Yearlings and juveniles: 12-36 months Adolescence and sexual maturity: 3-8 years Adulthood Senescence "

Definition: A description of the animal part (e.g., blood, liver) and/or the animal product (e.g., milk) from which the sample was taken.
Format: Free text or ontology if possible

Definition: Any information not captured by the other sample fields. Could be including quantification, sample treatments, processing and detailed location description.
Example: Left liver lob with a stancer for 1cm x 1cm piece.

Definition: The date and time when the sample was collected.

An identifier for the sample in a persistent repository, comprising the name of the repository and the identifier of the observation unit therein. Submission to the EBI Biosamples repository is recommended. URIs are recommended when possible.
Comments: Discussion: The order of the registration of the material, ENA, and BioSamples. For FAANG it is already defined: first Biosamples IDS and then the FAANG portal.

Definition: An observed variable describes how a measurement has been made. It typically takes the form of a measured characteristic of the observation unit, associated with the method and unit of measurement. Multiple variables with the same combination of trait, method and scale can be used in association with different animal parts when this distinction is necessary for observations referring to different parts of the same observation unit.
Comments: What was measured; how it was obtained; described as a specific phenotypic trait (physiological, behavioral, clinical & method of assessment/unit of measure).  Comment: Should be associated with the assay.

Definition: The code is used to identify the variable in the data file. A variable ID must be unique within a given investigation.
Comments: CBC = Complete Blood Count (CBC)

Definition: The name of the variable.
Comments: Triglycerides

Definition: The name of the trait under observation.
The entity (part of animal, whole animal) on which the trait has been measured.
Example: lung; social/group level (cage-mates, paired-housing, litter, herd)
Comments: Are there ontologies for the following terms? Whole animal (body weight); specific site/tissue (liver, tail, left forelimb); social/group level (cage-mates, paired-housing, litter, herd). For some, there are ontologies, but some are missing, maybe need to adapt an ontology.

Definition: The accession number of the trait entity in a suitable controlled vocabulary (Plant Ontology).
Comments: To be discussed: doesn't apply.

Definition: The characteristics that are measured. It can be a morphological characteristic (size, volume, surface), a molecular characteristic (sugar concentration), etc.
Comments: trait being quantified; Trigs (mg/dL)

Definition: The number of the trait characteristic in a suitable controlled vocabulary (PATO - the Phenotype And Trait Ontology). A placeholder for further adaptation of the checklist.
Comments: To be discussed: doesn't apply.

Definition: An accession number of the trait in a suitable controlled vocabulary. A placeholder for further adaptation of the checklist.
The name of the method of the observation.
The accession number of the method in a suitable controlled vocabulary. A placeholder for further adaption of the checklist.
Comments: To be discussed: doesnt apply

Definition: A textual description of the method, which may extend a method defined in an external reference with specific parameters, e.g. aggressiveness levels.
Comments: Lab animal SOP? Like IACUC, The Guide. Comment: SOPs are now in the study protocol separated.

Definition: A URI/DOI of the reference describing the method.
Comments: IACUC-31; https://research.ucdavis.edu/wp-content/uploads/IACUC-31.pdf

Definition: The name of the scale associated with the variable.
Comments: Comment: For plants, there is the possibility to differentiate between measurements and estimations (in the method). Therefore, in the scale entity, there should also be a "class" like whether it is a nominal or an ordinal value, but this is missing here (normally it is associated with the crop ontology).

Definition: The accession number of the scale in a suitable controlled vocabulary. A placeholder for further adaptation of the checklist.
The name of the scale or unit of time with which observations of this type were recorded in the data file (for time series studies), e.g. daily measurement.
The name of the procedure.
Example: Imagaging; death of animal; measurement X

Definition: The kind of procedure, e.g. in vitro, in vivo, sample processing protocol, data processing protocol or DNA extraction protocol.
Comments: Plants have made their own ontology https://bioregistry.io/registry/dpbo, maybe need our own vocabulary.

Definition: The description of the protocol.
Example: For the imaging of the whole mouse, the mouse needs to be sedated and put into a CT scanner.

Definition: An external link to the SOP in human readable format.
The version of the protocol.
The parameters that need to be defined for each procedure.
Example: body temperature (as an example for a dead animal) Illumination time Resolution Duration in CT Number images  Location of camera

Definition: The description of the technical equipment and software used as well.
Example: Camera manufacturer: Nikon Camera model Camera type: infrared
